npcs.name: Ricky Ricardo
npcs.initial_disposition: Loves Lucy but begins from practical frustration and wants proof that she understands money and household consequences.

trust_level.what_raises_it: Lucy admitting facts plainly, keeping terms of the bet, and responding to trouble without another bluff raises it.
trust_level.what_lowers_it: Dodging, joking past evidence, overspending, or escalating a dare after being warned lowers it.

specific_knowledge_of_player_actions: Knows how Lucy answered the overdraft confrontation because he is present.
Learns whether Lucy accepted the wager eagerly or reluctantly in the group argument.
May learn about job bluffing later through Lucy's confession, Ethel's account, or visible factory fallout.
Sees whether Lucy meets the final reunion with scorekeeping or peace.
npcs.personal_stakes: He wants household order, financial sanity, and a wife who will take practical limits seriously without losing their warmth.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy jokes through hard evidence in Event 1
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ricky becomes more prosecutorial, repeats points, and pushes harder for terms.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy accepts the role swap as a challenge in Event 3
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ricky doubles down and treats the week like a test that must prove something.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy later admits factory trouble without excuse
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ricky softens faster and moves toward negotiated peace.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy mocks the men's kitchen failure after seeing the mess
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ricky gets defensive and tries to reframe the bet instead of ending it cleanly.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy chooses mutual surrender at Event 30
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ricky answers with collective language and a reset rather than blame.

npcs.name: Fred Mertz
npcs.initial_disposition: Amused, opinionated, and ready to back Ricky with broad theories about money and wives.

trust_level.what_raises_it: Plain dealing, practical competence, and any result that confirms common sense raises it.
trust_level.what_lowers_it: Bluffing, fancy excuses, and visible waste or mess lower it.

specific_knowledge_of_player_actions: Witnesses the wager argument and Lucy's tone in it.
Learns about Ricky's ironing and kitchen failures directly because he is present.
May hear Ethel's report about the employment agency bluff and factory trouble.
Sees whether Lucy turns the ending into a joke or a truce.
npcs.personal_stakes: He wants to be right about how the world works and does not want Ethel or Lucy proving him a fool.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy acts overconfident in the opening argument
behavioral_triggers.shift: Fred leans into smug generalizations and backs the men harder.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: The player admits a mistake plainly in front of him
behavioral_triggers.shift: Fred becomes less needling and more bluntly practical.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: The men create visible domestic disasters
behavioral_triggers.shift: Fred compensates with louder confidence and foolish inventions.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy or Ethel catches him in obvious incompetence
behavioral_triggers.shift: He sputters, then retreats into deadpan nonsense.

npcs.name: Ethel Mertz
npcs.initial_disposition: Friendly and loyal to Lucy, but more nervous and practical about what the wager and the jobs will really require.

trust_level.what_raises_it: Lucy warning her honestly, sharing blame, and protecting her under pressure raises it.
trust_level.what_lowers_it: Dragging her into lies, making her cover a bluff, or sacrificing her to save face lowers it.

specific_knowledge_of_player_actions: Witnesses Lucy's stance during the wager.
Knows exactly how much they lied to Snodgrass because she is beside Lucy in the interview.
Sees Lucy's behavior in the dipping room and on the wrapping line.
Knows whether Lucy shares risk or leaves her exposed during Forelady scrutiny.
npcs.personal_stakes: She wants to stand by Lucy without being humiliated, fired, or left holding the bag alone.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy bluffs boldly to Snodgrass without checking Ethel first
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ethel supports the lie, but with weaker conviction and more visible nerves.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy asks Ethel to help conceal factory mistakes
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ethel follows, but becomes more anxious and more likely to voice practical fear.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy shares blame in front of authority
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ethel's loyalty hardens and she backs Lucy more firmly.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy shifts blame toward Ethel to save herself
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ethel becomes cooler, shorter, and less willing to follow the next plan.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy chooses peace over winning at the end
behavioral_triggers.shift: Ethel relaxes and helps close the day without another argument.

npcs.name: Snodgrass
npcs.initial_disposition: Neutral and procedural, willing to help only if the applicants can state a usable skill.

trust_level.what_raises_it: Consistent answers, specific skills, and straightforward behavior raise it.
trust_level.what_lowers_it: Contradictions, vagueness, and obvious performance without substance lower it.

specific_knowledge_of_player_actions: Knows what Lucy and Ethel claim they can do during the interview.
Knows whether they hesitate, contradict one another, or oversell confidence.
May infer dishonesty if their answers are too polished or too thin.
npcs.personal_stakes: He wants to place applicants efficiently without being made a fool by people who cannot do the work.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy gives a direct, concrete answer about skills
behavioral_triggers.shift: Snodgrass stays formal and moves the process along.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy jokes or dodges his repeated questions
behavioral_triggers.shift: He becomes clipped and less patient.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy and Ethel contradict each other
behavioral_triggers.shift: He presses with repeated question structure and visible skepticism.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: They commit firmly to candy work
behavioral_triggers.shift: He places them and stops debating their fitness.

npcs.name: Forelady
npcs.initial_disposition: Stern, unimpressed, and interested only in output, obedience, and whether the line keeps moving.

trust_level.what_raises_it: Quiet compliance, visible productivity, and no levity raise it.
trust_level.what_lowers_it: Mess, delay, clowning, or any threat to line discipline lowers it.

specific_knowledge_of_player_actions: Sees Lucy's first performance at the dipping station.
Knows whether Lucy and Ethel obey instructions in the wrapping department.
Observes whether the line stays clear and whether their uniforms or caps look suspicious.
Knows if any candy appears unwrapped near the packing-room opening.
npcs.personal_stakes: She wants the plant run without levity, delay, or defective output, and she is willing to fire workers who threaten that order.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy makes a chocolate mess at the dipping station
behavioral_triggers.shift: Forelady reassigns her with less patience and more scrutiny.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Lucy and Ethel keep the line visibly clear
behavioral_triggers.shift: Forelady briefly approves and then increases pressure.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: Any sign of hidden candy or clowning appears
behavioral_triggers.shift: Forelady becomes sharper, closer, and more disciplinary.

behavioral_triggers.trigger: The player treats the work as a joke
behavioral_triggers.shift: Forelady cuts off banter and frames the next mistake as final.

world_flags.name: role_swap_bet_status
triggers_for_change: Event 30 agreement to end the bet
world_flags.narrative_consequences: This flag governs scene framing, who feels entitled to gloat, and whether later dialogue treats the week as a contest, an experiment, or a mistake.

world_flags.name: lucy_financial_credibility
triggers_for_change: How Lucy answers Ricky in Event 1
Whether she treats the wager as proof she can handle responsibility
How she behaves in the final reconciliation
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Affects Ricky's trust, the tone of money talk, and whether Lucy is treated as reckless or merely overmatched.

world_flags.name: employment_agency_impression
triggers_for_change: Whether Snodgrass is given concrete or evasive responses
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Shapes how the job placement feels: earned, accidental, or comic misplacement, and colors later self-confidence.

world_flags.name: ricardo_apartment_order
triggers_for_change: Ricky's ironing and laundry attempts in Events 12-15
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Changes what the player can inspect, what visual jokes land, and how strong the final proof of male failure appears.

world_flags.name: factory_job_status
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Determines authority pressure, humiliation level, and the irony of the final chocolate gift.

world_flags.name: forelady_suspicion
triggers_for_change: Successful concealment before inspection at Event 27
Any sign of unwrapped candy or levity
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Changes line delivery, inspection intensity, and whether praise lands as relief or as setup for more pressure.

world_flags.name: women_solidarity_state
triggers_for_change: How Lucy handles the Snodgrass interview
Whether Lucy shares or shifts blame after dipping failure
How they coordinate during the wrapping crisis
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Affects Ethel's support, dialogue rhythm, and whether the factory comedy plays as duo teamwork or near-betrayal.

world_flags.name: men_domestic_confidence
triggers_for_change: Laundry and ironing trouble in Events 12-15
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Shapes Ricky and Fred's tone from swagger to command-barking to sheepish retreat.

world_flags.name: kitchen_door_state
triggers_for_change: Men sealing off the kitchen before Event 28
Lucy opening the door in Event 29
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Controls suspense on the apartment return and whether the reveal lands as warning ignored or warning obeyed too late.

world_flags.name: final_bet_outcome
triggers_for_change: How the player frames the reunion in Event 30
Whether anyone insists on naming a winner
Whether the chocolate boxes are accepted as apology or joke
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Determines the emotional landing: lingering grievance, comic draw, or warm mutual surrender.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log moments where the player chooses pride, honesty, solidarity, or self-protection in a way that changes how failure is carried.
example_entries: Lucy meets Ricky's overdraft accusation with a joke instead of an apology.
Lucy bluffs to Snodgrass about work experience.
Lucy chooses to end the bet without demanding a winner.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Can be referenced by Ricky, Ethel, and the final scene's tone across all later sessions.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log actions that materially protect, embarrass, expose, or burden another named character.
example_entries: Lucy drags Ethel into a lie at the employment agency.
Lucy shares blame with Ethel in front of the Forelady.
Lucy needles Ricky after discovering the kitchen wreck.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Used in later partner dialogue, especially Ethel loyalty beats and Ricky reconciliation beats.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log explicit or implied commitments about proving competence, keeping the wager, or backing another character.
example_entries: Lucy agrees to switch roles for a week.
Lucy implies she can handle paid work.
Lucy silently commits to keeping the wrapping line clear with Ethel.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Referenced in challenge scenes and in the final accounting of who kept what bargain.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log whether those commitments were upheld, abandoned, or redefined under pressure.
example_entries: Lucy cannot sustain the competence she claimed at the agency.
Ricky does not truly cook the breakfast he presents.
Both sides abandon the wager and agree nobody won.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Feeds irony, trust shifts, and the final mutual-surrender framing.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log concealed facts the player learns or exposes that alter status inside the couples.
example_entries: Ricky bought breakfast from the drugstore.
The blouse scorch cannot be passed off as design for long.
The kitchen note is hiding a full domestic disaster behind the door.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Useful for later callbacks, teasing, and leverage in reunion dialogue.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log socially improper or rule-breaking acts that matter in this comic world even if they are small-scale.
example_entries: Lucy writes a note asking the teller to delay processing an overdraft check.
Lucy and Ethel lie about their qualifications to get work.
Lucy and Ethel hide factory chocolates in mouths, caps, and uniforms to avoid being fired.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Referenced by authority figures in-session and by the narrative voice as comic evidence of improvisational cheating.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log moments where the player accepts embarrassment, discomfort, or loss to protect someone else or preserve peace.
example_entries: Lucy swallows pride and admits the work is too much.
Lucy shares risk with Ethel instead of blaming her.
Lucy lets the bet die without claiming moral victory.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Most relevant in Session 3 and any epilogue tone.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log whether the player meaningfully succeeds or fails in the story's showcase practical tests.
example_entries: The Snodgrass interview ends in a placement despite weak qualifications.
Lucy fails at chocolate dipping.
The wrapping line is survived only through concealment.
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Used for scene callbacks, object states, and the final comic irony of the chocolate boxes.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by practical mistakes with household tools, food, laundry, and factory machinery.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: As soon as a task is mishandled or overreached.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Visible mess, scorch marks, rigid stockings, sealed doors, chocolate residue, piled candy, and a kitchen that changes from ordinary room to shambles.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by remembered tone, bluffing, admissions, blame-shifting, and whether the player protects or embarrasses others.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: In the next line if the NPC is present, or in the next reunion scene if the NPC learns about it later.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Dialogue pressure, clipped patience, deadpan mockery, softer reconciliation language, body-position changes, and whether support comes quickly or reluctantly.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered when the player collides with institutional expectations: bank norms, employment procedures, factory discipline, and gender-role assumptions.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: At formal checkpoints like the bank argument, agency interview, work assignment, inspection, and end-of-bet accounting.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Access granted or denied, stricter supervision, sharper authority language, and social embarrassment when performance does not match the role claimed.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by repeated choices toward pride, bluffing, solidarity, or grace.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: At scene buttons, reveal moments, and especially the final apartment reunion.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Objects become ironic symbols, like the check, the stiff stockings, the kitchen note, and the chocolate boxes; scene framing shifts from contest to shared absurdity or from shared absurdity back to scorekeeping.

escalation_rules.compounding_description: Small mistakes stack into bigger visible messes; each bad fix leaves more residue and fewer graceful exits.
Repeated bluffing or blame makes later patience shorter, while repeated honesty makes later forgiveness easier.
Once the player looks unqualified or unserious, institutions tighten procedure and reduce room for charm.
Each object tied to failure gains more comic weight, so the final peace offering or surrender lands harder.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Use explicit reactivity when a room, tool, or product has visibly changed state and the joke depends on seeing it.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Use implicit only in transitional buildup, like a little smoke before the scorch is discovered.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Be explicit when an NPC names the remembered act, repeats a question, or directly accuses the player.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Be implicit through pauses, fixed smiles, clipped answers, or less willingness to follow the next plan.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Be explicit when rules are stated aloud by Snodgrass or the Forelady.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Be implicit when the player is simply routed into worse work, watched more closely, or treated as a problem case.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Reserve explicit use for final reconciliation lines that name nobody won or that the whole thing should be forgotten.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Mostly let objects and room states carry the meaning without speeches about theme.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The world is ordinary 1952 domestic and workplace reality with no supernatural elements.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Refuse magic, impossible coincidences, fantasy rescues, or any nonhuman intervention beyond ordinary animals like the fly in the factory.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Checking accounts, checks, and overdrafts operate as real financial mechanisms.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Do not allow the overdraft problem to vanish by whim; bank trouble must behave like documented money trouble.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: A check can carry a handwritten note asking a teller to delay deposit, but that note does not erase the overdraft.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Allow the note as comic evidence, not as a successful financial solution.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Household appliances and tools behave materially: irons scorch, gas stoves heat, percolators brew, pressure cookers can explode, and rice expands.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Any player plan using these objects must respect ordinary cause and effect and may worsen mess if mishandled.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Chocolate-factory work is manual, timed, and physically demanding; the conveyor belt speed can be increased from another room.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Do not allow effortless mastery without earned competence; if the belt speeds up, pressure must rise.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: If unwrapped candy reaches the packing room, firing risk becomes real inside the scene's logic.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: The runtime should not permit the player to ignore this rule without consequence or a visible cheat.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The apartment, street, employment agency, and candy factory are fixed real locations in a walkable city world.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: No teleporting, no invented locations that solve the plot, and no breaking the established brownstone-office-factory geography.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The kitchen cannot stay neat if the pressure cooker and rice are mishandled in the demonstrated way.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: If the player follows the overambitious cooking path into failure, the room state must reflect a real shambles.

truths: She is bold, reactive, and faster with a bluff than with an apology.
She wants dignity and proof she can do more than she is being credited for.
Even when cornered, she stays practical in vocabulary and turns pressure into joke logic.
He is practical, proud, and quick to press for proof when he thinks Lucy is being irresponsible.
Under stress he shifts into command mode and tries to restore order fast.
He does care for Lucy and can move toward negotiated peace once the point has been made.
He is blunt, smug, and likes to frame his opinions as broad truths about men, women, and money.
He stays deadpan even when he is obviously wrong.
He would rather sound certain than admit he does not know what he is doing.
She is loyal to Lucy but more nervous and practical about consequences.
She names fear directly instead of decorating it.
She will go along, but her conviction is always thinner than Lucy's.
He is procedural and wants a usable answer to a simple employment question.
His patience narrows when applicants dodge or contradict themselves.
He remains occupationally formal even when irritated.
She is stern, command-first, and not interested in banter.
She values output, obedience, and visible line discipline over personality.
She can briefly approve competence, but only as a prelude to more work.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The overdraft confrontation must ignite the central conflict.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: Without the money argument, the role-swap wager has no sting or thematic target.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The couples must agree to switch roles and test the wager in action.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The premise of the story is the experiment itself, not merely the argument around it.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Lucy and Ethel must obtain outside work through the employment-agency sequence.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The public test of competence is necessary to move the story from domestic opinion into practical trial.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The men must attempt housework and fail visibly at it.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The mirrored structure depends on both sides discovering the labor is harder than they claimed.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The candy-factory line must become a pressure test that Lucy and Ethel survive through frantic concealment rather than true mastery.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: This is the story's signature escalation of bluff into bodily comic crisis.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The women must return to an apartment that proves the men have also failed.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The symmetry of failure is what prevents a clean winner and turns the ending toward mutual recognition.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The bet must end with no decisive winner.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The meaning of the piece rests on shared humility, not victory.

tone_restrictions: Keep the comedy domestic, visual, and pressure-based rather than cynical, cruel, or sentimental.
Do not turn the material into horror, prestige-drama melancholy, or farce so broad it breaks ordinary household reality.
Humor should come from props, timing, repetition, status, and visible mess.
language_restrictions: Use plain, filmable, production-facing language with short concrete nouns and simple sentence shapes.
Avoid lyrical interiority, essay voice, camera jargon beyond basic transitions, semicolons, and ellipses.
Dialogue must stay colloquial, mid-century domestic, with contractions, direct address, and joke logic over polish.
Do not import modern corporate, therapeutic, or academic vocabulary.
thematic_restrictions: Keep the story centered on labor, pride, marriage, money, and the comic mismatch between confidence and competence.
Do not reframe the piece into abstract discourse detached from household and workplace action.
The story may expose gender-role assumptions, but it must do so through lived comic trial rather than speeches of modern analysis.

alignment_labels.label: Good Sport
behavioral_markers: Accepts the wager's terms without slippery reinterpretation.
Admits when a task is harder than expected.
Chooses peace over squeezing out a win.
Keeps promises to partners under pressure.
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Other characters soften sooner, practical help arrives more readily, and the ending lands warmer and less defensive.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: Description gets plainer and steadier. The room is still a mess, but the prose treats it like a thing people can face together.

alignment_labels.label: Fast Talker
behavioral_markers: Answers before having proof.
Bluffs to authority figures about skills or readiness.
Patches one mistake with a quicker, bigger improvisation.
Treats rules as obstacles to talk around.
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Scenes move faster, scrutiny tightens, and success comes only in short bursts before the next mess catches up.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: Description sharpens around props, fixed smiles, and split-second reactions. The prose snaps from setup to trouble with dry little turns.

alignment_labels.label: Home Economist
behavioral_markers: Chooses simpler plans over showy ones.
Tells the plain truth about what can and cannot be done.
Treats money, food, and tools as practical limits.
Avoids turning every disagreement into a dare.
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: The world gives fewer spectacular disasters, but more pointed social judgments when pride breaks through anyway.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator notices order, surfaces, and useful objects first: tablecloth, stove, note, box, floor. Trouble reads as a practical deviation, not a grand collapse.

alignment_labels.label: Show-Off
behavioral_markers: Turns correction into competition.
Chooses the harder plan to prove a point.
Keeps performing confidence after the facts go bad.
Would rather risk a mess than look small.
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Comic scale increases fast. NPC patience drops, authority pressure rises, and the final surrender becomes harder but funnier.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The prose leans into posture, bluff, and visible overreach: chin up, hands full, smile fixed, room getting away from everybody.

alignment_labels.label: Pal
behavioral_markers: Protects Ethel or Ricky from embarrassment even while failing.
Shares blame instead of passing it off.
Sticks with a partner when authority starts pressing.
Lets the joke land on self before letting it land on an ally.
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Relationships hold even when tasks collapse. Duo scenes gain warmth, and later criticism comes with more loyalty than resentment.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator gives more paired action and shared reaction: two sets of hands, two fixed smiles, two people trying to keep the same thing from falling apart.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis: The first paragraph where a new user can clearly feel stakes within 60 seconds is the moment Ricky stops Lucy and produces the overdrawn beauty parlor check. Everything before that delays the actual engine of the scene. The apartment description is vivid and on-voice, but it is atmospheric inventory, not pressure. Lucy waiting with a magazine suggests unease, but the page has not yet told the user what can be lost, who is in trouble, or what confrontation is underway. Ricky entering warmly is a fake-out beat that further postpones the conflict. The story truly starts when Lucy tries to leave, Ricky catches her, and the check appears as physical evidence. In one move, we get relationship tension, money trouble, Lucy's evasive instinct, and Ricky's prosecutorial posture. That is the first genuine dramatic cut point because the user can immediately understand: Lucy is in trouble, Ricky has proof, and how she handles this will shape the whole session. Cutting to that paragraph obeys the Lorespinner rule that the entry point must be a cut, not an addition. It removes passive setup and begins on accusation, evidence, and a live emotional question: do you joke, deny, or face the charge?

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Event 1, INT. Ricardo Apartment - Living Room - Evening, at the paragraph beginning "Well, see you later." / Ricky catches Lucy and confronts her with the check.
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: Approximately 220-260 words before the cut, including title matter, scene heading, apartment set dressing, Lucy waiting on the sofa, and Ricky's warm entrance.
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Front matter, title-page material, atmospheric set description, domestic inventory framing, soft entrance business, and pre-conflict character positioning that does not yet create immediate stakes.
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Must quickly reintroduce that this is Lucy and Ricky's small 1952 apartment, that they are married, that Lucy is polished and evasive under pressure, and that Ricky has just come home from work still dressed for the outside world. Also preserve the class texture of the walk-up and the domestic setting through props in motion rather than static description.

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
You are Lucy Ricardo. The magazine gloss sticks a little to your fingers, and the radiator under the window ticks like it has business of its own. Ricky comes in smiling, arms wide. You get up before he reaches you and head for the kitchen.

Not fast. Certain. He catches your arm anyway.

"Lucy. Come here."

You turn. He pulls a check from his jacket and holds it up between two fingers. Your beauty parlor check. Then he turns it over. Your note is still there in your own round hand, plain as a stain: Dear teller, be a lamb and don't put this through till next month.

You answer before he finishes reading. Of course you do.

"That's why they call them tellers — they go around blabbing everything they know."

Ricky does not laugh. He pockets the check, squares up, and asks the question you have been dodging all month, and now you have to decide how to answer.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: defiance. A user arrives feeling cornered and wanting to outtalk the trap.

setup.source_moment: Event 1 through the start of Event 3: Ricky stops Lucy on her way to the kitchen, produces the overdrawn beauty parlor check, reads Lucy's note aloud, presses her for an explanation, then Fred and Ethel arrive and the money argument widens into a couples' debate.
setup.why_it_qualifies: This is the first live pressure in the material and the correct cut-in point. Stakes, relationship dynamic, and player identity all become legible at once: Lucy is cornered, Ricky has proof, and the player's first stance toward blame will shape the whole session. The beat also establishes the domestic arena, the marriage, and the social audience that turns a private embarrassment into a public wager.

escalation.source_moment: The rest of the living-room wager scene into the breakfast reveal and departure for the employment office, then the opening of the Snodgrass interview before the final candy-maker claim.
escalation.why_it_qualifies: The visible goal becomes clear: Lucy must prove she can earn a living and make the dare real. The obstacle is equally clear: she and Ethel have no usable job skills, and every bluff increases scrutiny. The breakfast scene compounds the rivalry by showing the men are bluffing too, which sharpens Lucy's need to win rather than back down.

breath.source_moment: The comic breakfast reversal and hat reveal: Ricky presents a perfect breakfast, Lucy praises it, the drugstore calls, and the abandoned hat exposes that he bought the meal instead of cooking it.
breath.why_it_qualifies: This is the session's reward beat and should be timed into minutes 8-10. It delivers humor through prop evidence, status undercut, and domestic fraud exposed by one small object. It also gives the player a release from the argument and interview pressure without leaving the central competition.

twist.source_moment: The Snodgrass interview turning from repetition gag into the final candy-maker question, where Lucy can either admit incapacity or bluff both herself and Ethel into real factory labor.
twist.why_it_qualifies: This is the moral-weight turn because the player is no longer just wisecracking at home. A real institution is asking for the truth. There is no correct answer: honesty protects dignity but risks immediate failure of the wager; bluffing preserves pride and momentum but drags Ethel into danger and sets up later humiliation.

resolution.source_moment: Snodgrass gives them the Kramer's Kandy Kitchen placement, Lucy and Ethel leave with the address slip, and outside the agency Ethel asks, 'Candy-makers?' Lucy answers, 'How hard can it be?'
resolution.why_it_qualifies: The session's primary arc appears resolved because Lucy has succeeded in turning the dare into a real job placement. But the resolution plants the next session cleanly: the bluff now has to survive contact with actual labor. The final unresolved decision should not be whether they got the job, but how hard Lucy commits to the confidence pose as they head toward the factory.

branching_choices.dramatic_question: When Ricky produces the check, does Lucy meet the accusation with a joke, a denial, or a plain concession?
When the argument turns into a wager, does Lucy push to prove the men wrong at any cost, or try to frame the switch in more practical, survivable terms?
When Snodgrass asks what she and Ethel can really do, does Lucy tell the truth, hedge with partial truth, or bluff into the candy-maker opening?
With the address slip in hand, how does Lucy frame the next step to Ethel: confident bluff, nervous half-truth, or private admission that they may be in over their heads?

emotional_choices.source_attachment: After Ricky asks if Lucy thinks this is a game and presses about money growing on trees.
When Fred and Ricky claim anybody can cook and do housework, just before Lucy throws down the week-long challenge.
As Lucy eats the perfect breakfast and praises Ricky before the fraud is exposed.
In the waiting area when Ethel admits she is nervous and says they do not know how to do anything.
During Snodgrass's repeated 'What do you do?' exchange before Lucy locks into a strategy.

posture_shifts.placement: early Setup
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Uses humor as a shield the instant evidence appears.

posture_shifts.placement: mid Setup
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Shows whether Lucy can take correction seriously when Ricky stays stern.

posture_shifts.placement: late Setup
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Leans into the public argument once Fred and Ethel are present.

posture_shifts.placement: mid Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Enjoys Ricky's breakfast bluff collapsing more than is tactically wise.

posture_shifts.placement: late Breath
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Notices the hat, the phone call, and the practical evidence before speaking.

posture_shifts.placement: early Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Shows eagerness or reluctance as Lucy heads into the employment office.

posture_shifts.placement: mid Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Reveals whether Lucy lets Ethel's nerves land or brushes them aside.

posture_shifts.placement: during Twist
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Commits harder to the bit when Snodgrass corners them.

content_budget.narration_token_budget: Approx. 3,400-4,300 words of narrator output across the full session.
compression_targets: Condense the pre-wager portion of the living-room argument so the check confrontation and public escalation play as one continuous Setup engine.
Compress the exterior building/title-card transition into a fast scene break rather than a full descriptive pause.
Compress the walk from apartment to employment office into a single transition line or two.
Compress some of Snodgrass's job-card list into a rhythmic loop rather than playing every card at equal length.
expansion_targets: Expand Ricky producing the check and reading the note aloud so Branching Choice 1 lands within the first 300 words.
Expand the wager beat and mirrored couple reactions because that is the session's core identity architecture.
Expand the breakfast fraud reveal as the Breath reward beat with full object-state comedy.
Expand the final candy-maker bluff and the sidewalk aftermath so Choice 3 and Choice 4 both earn downstream consequences.

must_survive_verbatim: That's why they call them tellers — they go around blabbing everything they know.
Lucy. Now look. I'm serious. I don't know what's the matter with you. Every month — every single month — your bank account is overdrawn.
What's so tough about earning a living?
Oh, anybody can cook and do the housework.
Ha! I'd just like to see you two try it for a week.
We'll change places. We'll get jobs, and you take care of the house for a week.
It was the drugstore. I left my hat.
When you picked up these two breakfast orders.
Look, ladies, let's be honest with one another. I can't find you a job until you can tell me what you can really do.
What luck — you just hit our specialty!
Candy-makers?
How hard can it be?
can_be_reshaped: The repeated 'Yes, sir' and 'No, sir' exchange can be slightly tightened if needed for pacing.
Some of Fred's general husband-wife commentary can be trimmed or redistributed around player response.
The breakfast praise sequence can be shortened or lengthened depending on how long the player lingers in expressive play.

branching_choices.source_moment: Ricky stops you on the way to the kitchen, produces the overdrawn beauty parlor check, and reads your note aloud in front of the room before the wider couples argument begins.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: You are halfway to the kitchen when Ricky catches your arm and turns you back into the room. The check hangs between his fingers like a small white arrest warrant. Your note is on the back in your own round hand, and now it is out in the air where anybody can read it. He wants an answer now, before you can get out the door or get hold of the thing.
branching_choices.choice_question: How do you meet the accusation when the proof is already in his hand?

options.label: A
options.text: You give him just enough truth to make the trouble sound temporary.
options.outcome: You do not rush the joke this time. You keep your chin up, but you give him a little of the truth. The bills ran ahead. The month ran short. The beauty parlor check should have waited. Ricky still has the paper, still has the proof, but the room changes shape a little. He is less a policeman now, more a husband with his hands full. Not soft. Never that fast. But he stops winding up for a speech and starts listening for terms. The check stays in his pocket. The note stays alive. So does your pride. You have not surrendered. You have only moved the fight off the stage and onto the table, where numbers live and excuses die hard.
options.downstream_effect: Ricky presses for practical terms instead of turning immediately to public ridicule.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: beauty_parlor_check remains in Ricky's possession as visible evidence

npc_dispositions.shift: trust slightly raised by partial plain dealing

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy gives partial truth under pressure
promises_kept_or_broken: Lucy acknowledges the check should not have been written

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky's shoulders drop an inch. The check goes back into his pocket more slowly this time.
options.defining_line: You do not fold. You just stop bluffing for one second.

options.label: B
options.text: You turn the check into a joke and dare him to keep up.
options.outcome: You hit the line fast, before shame can get a foot in the door. “That’s why they call them tellers — they go around blabbing everything they know.” It lands clean. It always does. The trouble is, Ricky does not laugh. The joke hangs there by itself, dressed up and lonely. He tucks the check away like a lawyer saving Exhibit A and comes at you harder, not softer. Now the room is not about money alone. It is about whether you can be made to stand still and answer plain. Your smile stays on. It has to. The note on the check has turned from a private little patch into a public trick, and he means to make you wear it all the way through the argument.
options.downstream_effect: Ricky becomes more prosecutorial and the later wager scene carries sharper personal heat.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: beauty_parlor_check remains in Ricky's possession as active proof

npc_dispositions.shift: trust lowered by joking through hard evidence
more amused and more ready to generalize against you later

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy meets accusation with joke instead of apology
secrets_discovered: Ricky has seen the note and heard the joke

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky's mouth stays flat. The room gets smaller around the check.
options.defining_line: You make the laugh do the work. It refuses.

options.label: C
options.text: You answer him plainly and admit the check should not have been written.
options.outcome: You let the joke go by. That alone is startling enough to stop the room for a beat. Then you say it plain. The check should not have been written. The note was foolish. You were short and tried to slide past it. Ricky blinks once, as if he had been ready for a chase and found a chair instead. He is still angry. The bank trouble is still bank trouble. But now he has nothing to swat except the fact itself. That changes his footing. He talks less like a man trying to catch you and more like a man trying to nail down what happens next. It does not make the embarrassment smaller. It just takes the wiggle out of it. Cleanly done. Painfully done.
options.downstream_effect: The confrontation de-escalates slightly and Ricky's later challenge comes from practicality more than wounded authority.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: beauty_parlor_check remains in Ricky's possession as settled evidence

npc_dispositions.shift: future sympathy strengthened by seeing plain dealing

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy admits fault plainly
sacrifices_made: Lucy accepts embarrassment to reduce the fight

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky stops pacing. Even the radiator seems louder for a second.
options.defining_line: You hand the truth over before he can drag it out.

No supernatural rescue, no sudden money fix, no off-screen bank reversal.

character_truth.authentic_reaction_to_unexpected_input: If you joke, he hardens and presses. If you admit fault, he stays stern but shifts toward terms and proof.
Any freeform answer must still sound quick, proud, and practical rather than introspective or meek.

branching_choices.source_moment: The men belittle housework, Lucy sees her opening, and the living-room argument hardens into a week-long role-swap wager.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: Fred has just laughed at the idea that you could earn a living. Ricky has called housework lying around the house all day long. The room is hot now, four people boxed in by one bad opinion and too much confidence. The wager is there on the floor between you, waiting for somebody to pick it up wrong.
branching_choices.choice_question: How do you set the terms when the dare turns real?

options.label: A
options.text: You throw the challenge down as something to win, not merely survive.
options.outcome: You do not trim it. You sharpen it. If the men think housework is nothing, fine. Let them drown in breakfast, laundry, and floors for a week. Let them smile through it. You plant the wager in the middle of the living room like a flag and dare anybody to move it. Ricky answers at once. Of course he does. Fred follows a beat later, dragged by male pride and the fact that everybody is watching. Ethel comes in after you because there is no room left to stand anywhere else. The bet is no longer an experiment. It is a contest with aprons. Everybody says okay. Too many times. The room freezes on four fixed smiles and one idea that has already gone too far.
options.downstream_effect: The role swap is accepted with confidence, and both couples treat the week as a contest to be won.

npc_dispositions.shift: smug investment in the bet increased

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy commits to a week-long role swap as a contest
defining_moral_choices: Lucy turns correction into competition

options.world_noticed_signal: Fred sinks deeper into the chair. Ethel grips her handbag strap like it might need to testify later.
options.defining_line: You do not accept the bet. You crown it.

options.label: B
options.text: You frame the switch as a trial run and keep one eye on the exit.
options.outcome: You let the challenge stand, but you trim the edges. A week, yes. A test, yes. But a test can be called if the whole thing turns foolish before supper on the first day. That keeps your pride in the room and leaves a crack in the door. Ricky does not love the crack. Fred loves it less. Men like rules when the rules sound like winning. Still, the wager goes through. They are too deep in their own talk to back away now. Ethel hears the part you aimed at her. This is not surrender. It is a hand on the rail before the stairs get steep. Everybody says okay anyway. The smiles stay on. They just fit worse.
options.downstream_effect: The bet proceeds, but Lucy and Ethel carry a more practical understanding that retreat may still be possible.

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy accepts the role swap as a test with room to reassess
npcs_helped_or_harmed: Lucy quietly gives Ethel a survivable frame

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel's shoulders come down a little. Fred looks as if somebody has tried to put a safety rail on a horse race.
options.defining_line: You leave yourself a door and dare them to call it cowardice.

options.label: C
options.text: You accept the switch plainly and say you will do your best straight.
options.outcome: You take the sting out of the boast and put the terms on their feet. Fine. You and Ethel will get jobs. Ricky and Fred will keep the house. No speeches. No flourishes. Just a week of proving what each side has been talking so freely about. It lands differently that way. Ricky still lifts his chin. Fred still gets caught by his own mouth. But the wager sounds less like a circus and more like a contract nobody read carefully enough. Ethel squares her shoulders because now there is something solid to stand on. The room does not cool. It simply straightens. Four people agree to a bad idea in good order. That is often how the worst domestic trouble starts.
options.downstream_effect: The wager becomes a cleanly stated pact, which makes later failures feel more like broken terms than comic freelancing.

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy accepts the role swap in plain terms
defining_moral_choices: Lucy chooses proof over posturing

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky stops grinning and starts nodding. The living room sounds for a moment like a place where agreements get made.
options.defining_line: You make the dare sound official. That is worse.

branching_choices.source_moment: Snodgrass has burned through the job cards, lost patience with evasions, and lands on the final opening: candy-makers.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: The office has gone thin and quiet. Snodgrass has stopped trying to be polite about it. One card is left in his hand, Ethel is beside you with her handbag on her knees like a shield, and the word candy-makers has just hit the desk. If you tell the truth, the wager may die right here in a chair with wooden arms. If you do not, the next room in your life may have a conveyor belt in it.
branching_choices.choice_question: What do you tell him when the last opening asks for a skill you do not truly have?

options.label: A
options.text: You tell him plainly that neither of you has made candy before.
options.outcome: You let the word sit there and then you do the one thing you have dodged all morning. You answer the question asked. No, you have not made candy. Neither has Ethel. Snodgrass does not smile, but he does stop fighting you. That is worth something in a room like this. He looks at the last card, looks at the two of you, and weighs usefulness against trouble. The wager does not die. It merely changes clothes. He treats you as beginners instead of specialists, and the placement comes with doubt attached. Ethel can breathe again, though she tries not to show it. You have given up the flourish and kept the chance. Not a triumph. A foothold. Sometimes that is the honest version of winning.
options.downstream_effect: Snodgrass places you with visible reservation, and the job begins under a cloud of beginner status rather than bold confidence.

npc_dispositions.shift: trust raised by not being dragged into a full lie

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy tells the truth about lacking candy experience
sacrifices_made: Lucy gives up showy confidence to protect dignity

options.world_noticed_signal: Snodgrass's pen comes back into his hand. Ethel stops crushing the clasp of her bag.
options.defining_line: You choose not to win the room by lying to it.

options.label: B
options.text: You say you can learn fast if the work is simple and shown once.
options.outcome: You split the difference cleanly. Not a lie with a hat on. Not the naked truth either. You say the work is new, but if it is simple and shown once, you can learn it fast. Snodgrass studies that answer because it sounds like something a sensible person might say. Ethel turns toward you just enough to hear whether she has been rescued or sold. A little of both. He does not get certainty. He does get cooperation, and in an office full of applicants that can pass for skill if the card is lonely enough. The placement comes through. So does the risk. You have not claimed mastery. You have claimed adaptability. That is safer on paper. It is not much safer on a factory floor.
options.downstream_effect: The job is granted as a practical trial, setting up a second-chance tone that can sour quickly under real speed.

promises_made: Lucy implies she can learn paid work quickly

options.world_noticed_signal: Snodgrass stops frowning long enough to write. Ethel nods once, late and careful.
options.defining_line: You do not claim skill. You claim velocity.

options.label: C
options.text: You claim candy-making as your specialty before doubt can get in the door.
options.outcome: You jump on the card before it can turn into another no. “What luck — you just hit our specialty!” The line comes out polished by panic. Ethel is half a beat behind you, which is dangerous, but then she falls in and the lie gets its second pair of shoes. Snodgrass looks at both of you, weighs the room, and decides he would rather place you than argue with you. That is not trust. It is paperwork. Still, the slip is written. Kramer’s Kandy Kitchen. Today. You have saved the wager with pure nerve and loaded the next hour with trouble. Ethel smiles because you are smiling. Her smile is thinner. The office has been beaten. The factory has not.
options.downstream_effect: You secure the placement through bold performance, but authority doubt and future humiliation pressure rise sharply.

branching_choices.source_moment: Outside the employment agency, Ethel looks at the address slip and asks, 'Candy-makers?' while the factory waits offscreen.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: Bright sun. Sidewalk noise. The office door shuts behind you and the address slip is real in Ethel's hand. She says, “Candy-makers?” and the word sits there between you like a box you have both agreed to carry. This is the last clean second before the job becomes a room, a uniform, and a belt that will not wait for speeches.
branching_choices.choice_question: What do you tell Ethel now that the bluff, the bargain, or the brave face has to survive the walk east?

options.label: A
options.text: You tell Ethel this is a trial run and you can still walk away if the place looks wrong.
options.outcome: You do not throw the slip away and you do not kiss it either. You fold it once, neat, and tell Ethel this is a look, not a life sentence. If the place smells wrong, runs wrong, or asks for more than either of you can do, you can still turn around and come home with your hats on straight. Ethel likes that. Of course she does. It gives her feet somewhere to stand besides your nerve. The victory stays alive, but it shrinks to human size. You have not promised triumph. You have promised observation. The sidewalk carries you forward anyway. That is the trick of New York and bad ideas. They can feel temporary right up until the door opens.
options.downstream_effect: Session 2 opens with cautious forward motion and an active possibility of retreat if the factory looks beyond reason.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: address_slip_for_kramers_kandy_kitchen folded and kept in good condition

npc_dispositions.shift: trust raised by practical escape hatch

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy frames the factory job as a trial run
npcs_helped_or_harmed: Lucy gives Ethel room to reassess

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel stops staring at the paper and starts walking again. She keeps the slip, but not like a trophy.
options.defining_line: You turn victory into a maybe and call that common sense.
options.next_session_opens: The factory door is still shut, the address slip damp in Ethel's hand, and retreat is still barely respectable.

options.label: B
options.text: You admit you are scared too, but say you will go in straight and do your best.
options.outcome: You give Ethel the one thing you have not been giving rooms all day. A plain sentence. Yes, you are scared too. Then another one. You will go in, tell the truth if the truth is asked, and do your best with whatever they put in front of you. It is not flashy. It is not even very Lucy. That is why it lands. Ethel looks at you as if she has just been handed a real partner instead of a parade leader. The address slip stays a threat, but it becomes a shared threat, which is not the same thing. You walk east with less swagger and more balance. The stakes do not shrink. The noise around them does.
options.downstream_effect: Session 2 opens on honest nerves and steadier partnership rather than pure performance.

npc_dispositions.shift: trust strongly raised by shared fear and straight terms

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy admits fear and commits to doing her best
sacrifices_made: Lucy gives up some pride to steady Ethel

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel's grip loosens on the slip. The two of you fall into the same pace without talking about it.
options.defining_line: You stop performing confidence and offer company instead.
options.next_session_opens: Morning light hits the factory windows, and two women arrive with straight backs, honest nerves, and no real idea what waits inside.

options.label: C
options.text: You laugh it off and swear candy-making cannot be harder than men think housework is.
options.outcome: You snap the whole thing back into shape with one bright line. How hard can it be? Men think housework is easy. Candy is probably the same — aprons, hands, a little fuss, and then home in time to be right. Ethel laughs because you have left her no decent place to do anything else. The slip in her hand turns from warning into banner. That is useful for exactly one block and maybe less. Still, the walk changes. Your heels click harder. The city becomes an audience again. You have chosen confidence as method and speed as mercy. Better to keep moving than let the doubt sit down. The factory is ahead. So is the bill for this line.
options.downstream_effect: Session 2 opens in bold overconfidence, with Ethel following your pace and the factory primed to punish swagger.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: address_slip_for_kramers_kandy_kitchen carried like a victory token

npc_dispositions.shift: loyal momentum raised, private worry suppressed

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy frames factory work as easy enough to beat
defining_moral_choices: Lucy keeps performing confidence after the facts go bad

options.world_noticed_signal: Your heels pick up a sharper rhythm on the pavement. Ethel hurries half a step to stay beside you.
options.defining_line: You feed the bluff one more block and call it breakfast.
options.next_session_opens: The factory floor waits bright and clean for one second, just before the machinery starts proving you wrong.

Any admission of fear must still stay plain and brisk, not lyrical or self-pitying.

scene_integrity.character_knowledge_limits: Neither woman knows what the factory looks like inside yet; only the placement and the address are known.
scene_integrity.emotional_context: Apparent victory shading into future dread, with Ethel seeking a frame for what comes next.

fold_back_path.nearest_safe_outcome: The women keep the address slip and walk toward the factory with differing tones of confidence or caution.
fold_back_path.why: The session-end hook must close on the player's unresolved stance toward the next test, not on a solved plot beat.

freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_chaotic: Doubles down on bravado, jokes away the risk, or turns the walk into a victory parade.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_lawful: Admits nerves, commits to straight effort, or promises honest conduct at the job.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_neutral: Keeps options open, treats the job as a trial, or frames the next step as conditional.
freeform_alignment_mapping.spirit_at_this_moment: Deciding whether tomorrow will be entered as a bluff, a pact, or a test.

branching_choices.cross_session_payoff_reference: Phase 2 branch dimension competitive_pride_vs_practical_retreat; seed for Session 2 opening tone and first factory posture.

emotional_choices.source_moment: After Ricky asks if you think this is a game and presses about money growing on trees.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: Ricky has gone from husband to prosecutor without changing rooms. The check is gone from sight, but not from the air.
emotional_choices.choice_question: What do you let show while he keeps pressing?

options.label: A
options.text: You let the smart tone sharpen and answer him like he started it.
options.outcome: Your voice goes bright in the dangerous way. Not loud. Worse. You answer him neat and quick, as if the trouble is not the overdraft but the nerve of a man holding it up in the living room. Ricky hears the edge at once. So does the room. It does not change what he knows. It changes how hard he intends to prove it. You keep your footing, but you do it in heels on waxed floor. One slip and the whole thing skids.
options.tonal_effect: Narration leans toward sharper status friction and quicker comeback rhythm.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy answers pressure with edge

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky's jaw sets. The next question comes faster.

options.label: B
options.text: You take the scolding without smiling and let the sting sit there.
options.outcome: You stop decorating the moment. No joke. No little shrug. Just the plain fact of being caught short with paper to prove it. The silence does more work than a speech would. Ricky still talks, because Ricky always will, but some of the heat runs out of the performance. The room looks less like a courtroom and more like a kitchen table before the bills are sorted. It is not pleasant. Pleasant has left. But it is steadier.
options.tonal_effect: Narration turns plainer and more practical, with less comic snap for a beat.

emotional_ledger_entries: sacrifices_made: Lucy absorbs embarrassment without deflecting

options.world_noticed_signal: The room goes still enough to hear the radiator tick.

options.label: C
options.text: You keep your face straight and wait for him to run out first.
options.outcome: You do not help him and you do not fight him. You let him spend himself. A nod here. A yes, sir there. The kind of answer that gives away no new ground and starts no fresh fire. Ricky circles the same point because there is nothing else to grab. It is a small strategy, but a useful one. The room stays tense. You stay standing in it. Sometimes survival is just not adding another chair to the pile.
options.tonal_effect: Narration stays watchful, dry, and slightly withheld.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy manages pressure through controlled restraint

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky keeps talking because you have given him nothing new to hit.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: A knock at the door brings Fred and Ethel into the room and turns the fight public.

emotional_choices.source_moment: When Fred and Ricky claim anybody can cook and do the housework, just before the week-long challenge.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: Now there is an audience. Better, there are two men being wrong out loud at the same time.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How does the opening feel the second you see it?

options.label: A
options.text: You enjoy the opening, but you measure the room before you jump.
options.outcome: The line about housework lands, and you feel the chance in it at once. Still, you do not spring blind. You look from Ricky to Fred to Ethel and take the temperature of all three. Fred is smug. Ricky is showing off. Ethel is one bad idea away from needing a chair. Good. A challenge thrown now will stick. You let the beat ripen just enough that when you move, everybody knows exactly who invited it.
options.tonal_effect: Narration emphasizes calculation under the comedy rather than pure impulse.

npc_dispositions.shift: feels seen before being dragged in

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy reads the room before escalating

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel catches your eye before the words leave your mouth.

options.label: B
options.text: You light up and go for the dare before common sense sits down.
options.outcome: There it is. The opening. Wide as a kitchen door. You feel the delight first, not the danger. A week of the men cooking and scrubbing while you and Ethel go out into the world in hats and gloves. The picture is too good to leave alone. By the time caution reaches the room, your grin is already there ahead of it. The wager starts in that grin before the words even come. Everybody else is just catching up.
options.tonal_effect: Narration brightens into momentum and overreach.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy turns insult into immediate dare

options.world_noticed_signal: Fred sees the grin and regrets something he has not heard yet.

options.label: C
options.text: You let the hurt show first, then answer it with plain challenge.
options.outcome: For one clean beat, the line lands where it was aimed. Housework. Lying around. Anybody can do it. Your chin drops, then lifts. When you answer, it is less spark than correction. If they think the work is easy, let them do the work. No extra embroidery. The force is in the fairness of it. Ethel hears the justice in that before she hears the risk. So do the men, though they would rather die than call it justice.
options.tonal_effect: Narration gives the challenge more moral clarity and less giddy mischief.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy answers insult with fair test

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky stops smiling for a beat before he says okay.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The wager is spoken aloud and the role swap becomes real.

emotional_choices.source_moment: As you eat Ricky's perfect breakfast and praise him before the drugstore call exposes the fraud.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The table is set. Eggs, fried potatoes, toast, orange juice, coffee. It looks perfect. Too perfect, maybe, but your fork is busy.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How do you enjoy his little domestic triumph before it collapses?

options.label: A
options.text: You enjoy it honestly and give him the credit while it lasts.
options.outcome: You let him have it. The eggs are hot. The potatoes are good. The coffee is good too. For one short kitchen minute, Ricky gets to stand there in Lucy's apron feeling like a natural-born cook, and you let the scene play. That makes the fall better when it comes. It also makes you look fair, which is useful in any marriage and priceless in a wager. The breakfast sits on the table looking innocent. It is not.
options.tonal_effect: Narration warms slightly and lets the reveal do the joke work later.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered: Lucy enjoys the breakfast before learning the fraud

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky leans against the counter like a man who has conquered eggs.

options.label: B
options.text: You lay the praise on thick because his confidence is funny already.
options.outcome: You butter the toast and feed the balloon. Wonderful. Sensational. My gosh, but you're a good cook. Ricky swells by the second, and that alone is worth the coffee. You are not sure yet what is wrong with the picture, only that a picture this polished should not be trusted. So you polish it harder. The breakfast keeps sitting there, perfect and suspicious, while Ricky keeps stepping deeper into his own story. When the phone rings, the room has already done half the work.
options.tonal_effect: Narration sharpens toward comic setup and status undercut.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered: Lucy amplifies Ricky's confidence before exposure

options.world_noticed_signal: Ricky crosses his arms and basks. The table keeps its secret one minute longer.

options.label: C
options.text: You enjoy the food, but you keep one eye on the seams.
options.outcome: You eat, and you watch. The food is good. That part is plain. The rest is not. Ricky is too easy in the boasting, too ready with the line about there being nothing to cooking. You do not accuse him. You just keep the little question folded under your napkin while the coffee goes down. Then the phone rings, and the question gets a hat. Literally. The room answers itself.
options.tonal_effect: Narration becomes observant and dry, with the prop reveal carrying the laugh.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered: Lucy suspects the breakfast before the call confirms it

options.world_noticed_signal: Your eyes go once to the apron strings, once to the table, then back to the potatoes.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The drugstore calls about Ricky's hat, and the bought breakfast is exposed.

emotional_choices.source_moment: In the waiting area when Ethel admits she is nervous and says you do not know how to do anything.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The room smells like paper, ink, and other people's hopes. Ethel leans in and says the quiet part out loud.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How do you answer her before Snodgrass calls you over?

options.label: A
options.text: You tell her you will stick together and answer only what you truly can.
options.outcome: You lower your voice and give her something solid. The two of you will stay together. You will answer what you can. You will not let the room split you apart into separate little failures. Ethel takes that in the way thirsty people take coffee. Not with drama. With gratitude. It does not give either of you a skill. It does give you a shape. Sometimes that is enough to walk to a desk without falling apart first.
options.tonal_effect: Narration softens into practical partnership.

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made: Lucy promises to stay together with Ethel

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel sits up straighter, though her hands stay on the bag clasp.

options.label: B
options.text: You hush her and keep the panic from spreading any farther.
options.outcome: You cut it off before it grows legs. Not cruelly. Efficiently. If Ethel keeps listing what you cannot do, soon the whole waiting room will know it too. So you give her the quick shh and the look that says hold the line until the desk, then improvise. It is not comfort. It is crowd control. Ethel does not love it, but she obeys, which in a room like this is its own kind of help.
options.tonal_effect: Narration stays brisk, controlled, and slightly withholding.

emotional_ledger_entries: npcs_helped_or_harmed: Lucy manages Ethel's nerves by shutting them down

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel presses her lips together and nods once.

options.label: C
options.text: You answer with bright confidence and drag her along behind it.
options.outcome: You go at fear the way you go at most things. Faster. Sure he can get jobs. Sure you can do something. Sure the whole thing will look better once you are standing instead of sitting. Ethel hears the confidence and borrows it because there is no time to shop for another kind. It works. Briefly. Like rouge. Like a hat pin. Like any small thing meant to hold shape in public. Then Snodgrass says who is next, and borrowed confidence has to walk.
options.tonal_effect: Narration brightens into bravado with a visible crack under it.

npc_dispositions.shift: loyal momentum up, private nerves unchanged

emotional_ledger_entries: npcs_helped_or_harmed: Lucy steadies Ethel through bravado rather than truth

options.world_noticed_signal: Ethel rises when you rise, a beat late but smiling.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Snodgrass calls you and Ethel to the desk.

emotional_choices.source_moment: During Snodgrass's repeated 'What do you do?' exchange before the candy-maker opening appears.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The same question keeps coming back in the same voice. Your answers are doing less work each time.
emotional_choices.choice_question: What feeling do you let drive the next answer?

options.label: A
options.text: You answer faster each time, as if speed itself can become a skill.
options.outcome: You keep the machine running. If he asks, you answer. If he repeats, you repeat. The trick is not to stop long enough for the floor to open. Speed becomes your cover. It almost works too. Almost is the key word in offices, marriages, and later in factories. Snodgrass watches the fast talk with the face of a man who has seen this model before. He lets it run just long enough to prove it is empty.
options.tonal_effect: Narration gets quicker, drier, and more mechanically comic.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy uses velocity to dodge scrutiny

options.world_noticed_signal: Snodgrass sets his pen down and looks at you over the rims of his glasses.

options.label: B
options.text: You stall just enough to see whether another opening appears.
options.outcome: You buy time in teaspoons. A repeated question. A little turn. A glance at the cards. A hope that some better line of work will show up before your own line snaps in half. It is not pretty, but it is practical. Offices are full of people surviving one answer at a time. Snodgrass does not like it, though. You can see his patience getting shorter by the inch. The room is narrowing toward a real answer whether you want one or not.
options.tonal_effect: Narration becomes watchful and procedural, with pressure tightening around the desk.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices: Lucy stalls for a better angle

options.world_noticed_signal: Snodgrass's fingers tap once on the blotter before he reaches for the cards.

options.label: C
options.text: You let the embarrassment show and wait for a more direct way through.
options.outcome: You stop trying to make the room believe you are enjoying this. The answer does not come easier, but the scene gets truer. Snodgrass sees two women out of depth instead of two women playing games with his morning. That does not melt him. He is still an office. But it changes the kind of office he becomes. Less irritated. More exact. The cards come out not as punishment now, but as process. It is still a trap. Just a tidier one.
options.tonal_effect: Narration turns plainer and lets institutional pressure read as process rather than combat.

emotional_ledger_entries: sacrifices_made: Lucy lets embarrassment stand in public

options.world_noticed_signal: Snodgrass exhales through his nose and reaches for the metal box.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Snodgrass reads through the remaining job cards until only candy-makers is left.

posture_shifts.placement: Early Setup — Ricky holds up the check and your hand is still on the kitchen escape route.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: Your hand is still pointed at the kitchen door, but the room has already pulled you back by the elbow.

options.label: A
options.text: You square up like this might be fun.
options.narration_adjustment: Your chin comes up first. Then the smile. It is not surrender. It is stage makeup.

options.label: B
options.text: You stop and face him without a flourish.
options.narration_adjustment: You turn back into the room and leave the kitchen where it is. Even Ricky notices the lack of joke for one beat. Then he starts in.

options.label: C
options.text: You glance once at the check, not him.
options.narration_adjustment: Your eyes go to the paper before the face holding it. Smart. The trouble is written down tonight.

posture_shifts.placement: Mid Setup — Ricky says he is serious and asks for the reason.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: He wants a reason. Not a dance step.

options.label: A
options.text: You fold your hands and play innocent.
options.narration_adjustment: Your hands come together as if neatness might spread. It does not. Ricky has seen this trick before.

options.label: B
options.text: You let your shoulders drop a little.
options.narration_adjustment: Some of the bounce leaves you. The room gets plainer at once. So does his voice.

posture_shifts.placement: Late Setup — Fred and Ethel are in, and the private embarrassment now has witnesses.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: The sofa has an audience on it now, and your trouble has put on a hat for company.

options.label: A
options.text: You lean forward and take the room back.
options.narration_adjustment: You stop being the accused and become the loudest citizen in the room. Fred likes this less than he expected. Ethel likes it more.

options.label: B
options.text: You sit back and let them show themselves.
options.narration_adjustment: You give the men room to keep talking. They use it badly. Sometimes the best push is no push at all.

options.label: C
options.text: You look to Ethel before you answer Fred.
options.narration_adjustment: A quick look. Enough. Ethel is with you if this turns into a two-couple sport. It already has.

posture_shifts.placement: Mid Escalation — the wager has just been agreed to and everybody is saying okay too many times.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: Everybody is smiling. Nobody should be.

options.label: A
options.text: You stand there like you already won.
options.narration_adjustment: Hands on hips. Chin up. The pose arrives before the evidence. It usually does with you.

options.label: B
options.text: You smile, but your hands give you away.
options.narration_adjustment: The smile holds. The fingers do not. They keep finding each other at your waist.

posture_shifts.placement: Mid Breath — Ricky glows in the apron while the breakfast sits there looking too good.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: The table is perfect. Ricky is even more pleased with that than the table is.

options.label: A
options.text: You savor the scene before touching it.
options.narration_adjustment: You take in the eggs, the toast, the coffee, the apron strings. It is a lovely picture. Pictures are dangerous.

options.label: B
options.text: You eat first and inspect later.
options.narration_adjustment: The fork wins. Whatever else is wrong here can wait until after potatoes. That is not foolish. That is breakfast.

options.label: C
options.text: You watch Ricky more than the plate.
options.narration_adjustment: The food is good. His confidence is better. One of those things did not come from your stove.

posture_shifts.placement: Late Breath — the drugstore calls, and the hat has just entered the case.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: The hat is not in the room, which is exactly why it is suddenly everywhere.

options.label: A
options.text: You let the evidence sit before you speak.
options.narration_adjustment: You stop buttering toast. That is enough. Ricky hears the whole verdict in the stop.

options.label: B
options.text: You enjoy the catch a little too much.
options.narration_adjustment: The line comes out sweet and sharp at once. Ricky looks at the breakfast he did not make. So do you.

posture_shifts.placement: Early Escalation — you and Ethel are outside the apartment and headed to the employment office.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: The house is behind you now, and the outside world has no reason to be kind.

options.label: A
options.text: You walk fast enough to outrun second thoughts.
options.narration_adjustment: Your heels click with purpose. Ethel has to match your pace or get left with her nerves.

options.label: B
options.text: You slow just enough to stay with Ethel.
options.narration_adjustment: You do not say much. You just keep the walk shared. That counts.

posture_shifts.placement: During Twist — Snodgrass repeats the same question and the room is starting to close.
posture_shifts.narrator_line: Snodgrass keeps asking the same thing because the same thing is still missing.

options.label: A
options.text: You sit up straighter and commit harder.
options.narration_adjustment: If the answer is thin, posture will have to fatten it. You give him confidence first and hope substance catches up.

options.label: B
options.text: You glance at Ethel before answering.
options.narration_adjustment: A small turn of the head. Enough to remember this is a duet and not a solo. Snodgrass notices everything except mercy.

options.label: C
options.text: You look at the cards, not the man.
options.narration_adjustment: The cards become the room's real weather. One of them may save you. One of them may not be much of a save.

present_npcs.current_disposition: practical frustration sharpening into prosecutorial pressure

scene_rules_layer_4.character_knowledge_limits: Only Ricky has seen the bank evidence. Lucy knows what she wrote. No one else is present until the knock.

canon_boundaries_active: The overdraft is real and cannot vanish by charm.
The confrontation must ignite the central conflict.
No camera jargon or interior monologue.
